heading: Abstract
content: Deploying LLMs on mobile devices faces a tradeoff between underperforming small models and resource-hungry large ones. The paper shows mobile NPUs have underutilized computational resources, particularly matrix multiplication units, during typical LLM inference, because GEMM degenerates into GEMV during decoding. The authors propose applying parallel test-time scaling on mobile NPUs to enhance smaller LLMs using this wasted compute. Two hardware challenges block this: inadequate NPU support for fine-grained quantization, and low efficiency of general-purpose vector computations. Two techniques solve these: a hardware-aware tile quantization scheme aligning group quantization with NPU memory access patterns, and efficient LUT-based replacements for complex operations like Softmax and dequantization. The resulting end-to-end inference system targets Qualcomm Snapdragon platforms and achieves up to 19.0x speedup for mixed-precision GEMM and 2.2x for Softmax, with smaller models using test-time scaling matching or exceeding larger models' accuracy, achieving a new performance-cost Pareto frontier. Authors: Zixu Hao, Jianyu Wei, Tuowei Wang, Minxing Huang, Huiqiang Jiang, Shiqi Jiang, Ting Cao, Ju Ren (Tsinghua University, USTC, Microsoft Research), EuroSys 26, arXiv 2509.23324v1, CC BY 4.0.

heading: Introduction
content: Mobile-tailored LLMs (Llama 3.2, MiniCPM, Gemma) generally underperform larger counterparts, and scaling model size increases memory/bandwidth demands beyond resource-constrained mobile platforms. Test-time scaling improves accuracy by spending more compute at inference time instead of changing model parameters, historically limited to cloud/offline settings with abundant compute. Key insight: mobile NPUs (Qualcomm Hexagon NPU claims 45 TOPS INT8 in Snapdragon X Elite; AMD NPUs improved 3.1x across generations) achieve high peak performance via dedicated matrix multiplication units on large matrix tiles, but in typical LLM inference GEMM degenerates into GEMV during decoding (one token processed at a time), causing low hardware utilization. This underutilization is exactly the free compute test-time scaling (increased sampling parallelism/batch size) can consume without substantially adding inference overhead. Two hardware challenges: Precision (mobile NPUs lack native fine-grained group quantization support, and per-channel quantized models suffer severe degradation on reasoning tasks critical to test-time scaling) and Efficiency (NPUs excel at matrix multiplication but general-purpose vector units have limited throughput/bandwidth, becoming a bottleneck for non-matrix computations, worsened by a mismatch between wide SIMD vector components and data granularity). Contributions: analysis of NPU matrix-unit underutilization during decoding; two techniques (hardware-aware tile quantization, LUT-based computation) to accelerate test-time scaling on mobile NPUs; an end-to-end inference system with minimal proprietary software dependency; demonstration that test-time scaling achieves Pareto-frontier performance/cost. Code at github.com/haozixu/llama.cpp-npu (main repo) and github.com/haozixu/htp-ops-lib (op library).

heading: Background: Test-Time Scaling and NPUs
content: Parallel test-time scaling improves accuracy without modifying model parameters by devoting more computation at test-time. Simplest methods are majority-voting and self-consistency, selecting the most consistent answer across multiple generated samples. For domains with verifiable outcomes or Outcome Reward Models, Best-of-N selects the highest-scoring completion from a full sample set. Via lookahead rollouts similar to Monte Carlo Tree Search, Process Reward Models can directly score intermediate results, enabling lookahead-free step-level Beam Search that dynamically discards low-quality generation paths. Modern SoCs increasingly integrate NPUs for low-precision, computationally-intensive neural network inference (e.g. GEMM) at high throughput and good power efficiency. A widely adopted architecture pairs a matrix unit (matrix multiplication/convolution) with a vector unit (normalization, activation functions), seen in Qualcomm Hexagon, Huawei Ascend, AMD XDNA, Intel NPU, and Intel Gaudi HPU. NPUs differ from GPUs: GPU SIMT lets threads independently branch/access-memory/compute, while NPU SIMD has a single thread operate on large vector/matrix data blocks; NPUs use fewer hardware threads and VLIW architectures, trading programming flexibility for execution/energy efficiency.

heading: Qualcomm's Hexagon NPU Architecture
content: The Hexagon NPU is programmed primarily via Qualcomm AI Engine Direct (QNN), a proprietary closed-source DNN inference framework; developers generally cannot customize low-level kernels because the matrix unit's instructions are undisclosed, even though the Hexagon SDK ships a full LLVM toolchain. The authors reverse-engineered the undocumented FP16 matrix-unit instructions from binary libraries to use them directly. Architecture is a hybrid vector plus matrix design: HVX (Hexagon Vector eXtension) is the vector unit with 6-8 scalar VLIW hardware threads, 32 vector registers of 1024 bits each, and 4-6 HVX unit instances; HMX (Hexagon Matrix eXtension) is the matrix unit with 1-2 instances supporting INT4/INT8/INT16/FP16 precisions. Memory subsystem: shared 1 MiB L2 cache plus 8 MiB TCM (Tightly Coupled Memory, software-managed on-chip); HVX reads from either, but HMX and vector scatter/gather can only access TCM; DDR-to-L2 via l2fetch (20-30 GB/s), DDR-to-TCM via DMA (about 60 GB/s), both supporting async 1D/2D tensor transfers. The HMX unit's basic data unit is a tile: for FP16 HMX, a tile is 32x32 elements (2 KiB); HMX loads weight/activation tiles from TCM, multiplies tile pairs, accumulates internally, and can independently scale/bias each output channel. FP16 HMX tiles use a special memory layout where every two rows are permuted, matching a transposed 2x32 sub-matrix; weight tiles are stored column-major since HMX performs inner-product at tile level.

heading: Opportunity: Free Matrix Computation During LLM Decoding
content: During autoregressive generation, input is typically one token, so GEMM degenerates into GEMV: a [1, hidden_dim] activation times [hidden_dim, proj_dim] weights becomes an effective [1,32]x[32,32] HMX tile computation where 31 of 32 input-tile rows are wasted padding, meaning low matrix-unit utilization. Meanwhile test-time scaling methods (Self-Consistency, Best-of-N, Beam Search) explore multiple generation paths with batch size greater than 1; as the generation budget (max batch size) increases, accuracy on MATH500 improves significantly for both Llama3.2-1B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct in the paper's own measurements. This motivates running test-time scaling on mobile NPUs: otherwise-wasted matrix-unit compute gets used, decoding overhead shouldn't increase much in theory, and generation quality improves at runtime without touching model weights.

heading: Challenges: Precision and Efficiency
content: Insufficient Precision: HMX supports FP16 GEMM but full-precision models are impractical on-device, requiring quantization, yet mobile NPU matrix units (including HMX) were designed for coarse-grained (per-tensor/per-channel) quantization, and QNN's software stack only supports these coarse schemes. The paper's own measurement of Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct under QNN per-channel quantization vs AutoAWQ per-group 4-bit quantization (both W4A16) shows QNN's coarse quantization severely degrades reasoning accuracy: MATH500 2.1% (QNN) vs 15.9% (AutoAWQ); GSM8K 3.4% vs 32.6%; Wikitext perplexity 28.99 vs 19.42 (lower better). Since test-time scaling relies on baseline accuracy in exactly these reasoning tasks, QNN's quantization fails minimal requirements. Weak General-Purpose Compute and Memory Bandwidth: measured on Hexagon V75 with a 1024x1024x1024 GEMM (all data in on-chip TCM), HMX reaches 12,032.54 GFLOPs FP16 GEMM vs HVX (1 thread) at only 32.93 GFLOPs, over 300x difference. DMA read bandwidth exceeds 60 GB/s vs the vector unit's core-data-path read bandwidth under 30 GB/s; DMA's high bandwidth is restricted to large regular 1D/2D blocks and can't efficiently handle small/irregular accesses. This gap is the central engineering obstacle for fine-grained-quantized mixed-precision GEMM.

heading: Design Overview
content: To address accuracy, the system adopts 4-bit fine-grained group quantization for primary weights while keeping activations in floating-point, dynamically dequantizing weights to floating-point at runtime using the NPU's FP16 matrix capability. For the resulting general-purpose-compute burden, the strategy is hardware-aware offline design to minimize runtime overhead, and fully exploiting SIMD vector-unit capabilities. Two specific techniques: Hardware-aware Fine-grained Tile Quantization Scheme, a novel quantization layout performing group quantization in fine-grained rectangular tiles rather than along the conventional accumulation axis, via an offline pipeline of weight pre-quantization transformation, quantization, and post-quantization transformation, improving runtime memory access continuity and eliminating unnecessary compute overhead; and Efficient LUT-Based Computation, replacing complex runtime operations (exponential computation in Softmax, dequantization in mixed-precision GEMM) with vector-unit lookup-table instructions.

heading: System Design: Hardware-Aware Tile Quantization
content: Two problems with naive fine-grained quantization on NPUs: mismatch between the matrix unit's expected weight layout and conventional group-quantization layout (elements contiguous in conventional column-major layout become scattered in on-chip TCM when reorganized for HMX tile layout, and SIMD gather/scatter fixes remain expensive; simple transposition doesn't resolve it), and suboptimal vector-register utilization from small group sizes. Tile-Group Quantization: weights are first permuted into the HMX-expected layout, then round-to-nearest quantization applied group-by-group; for group size 32, this performs group quantization in units of 2x16 tiles. Since pretrained weights approximately follow a zero-mean Gaussian distribution, this reshaping doesn't significantly change per-group statistical properties, so quantization error stays comparable to conventional grouping. Coalescing Quantization Groups for Wide Vector Access: quantized weights default to Array-of-Structures layout (e.g. Q4_0: each 32-element group is 16 bytes INT4 values plus 2 bytes FP16 scale, interleaved) to match the NPU's software-managed local prefetching, but a single 32-element quantization group is too small to fill a 128-byte-wide vector register. Fix: coalesce 8 quantization groups into a super-group, reorganizing so 256 consecutive INT4 elements fit exactly into one full HVX register.

heading: System Design: LUT-Based Computations
content: Fast Softmax via Vector Gather: profiling FlashAttention on the Hexagon NPU (Qwen2.5-1.5B, prompt length 4096) shows GEMM contributes little latency while Softmax dominates as query length grows, bottlenecked by exponential computation which must run on HVX (no dedicated math-function hardware). The authors explore a precomputed LUT via HVX's vgather instruction rather than the standard exp-to-exp2 substitution. Storing a full 32-bit-float LUT is impractical, and vgather itself costs 24-48 instruction packets on Hexagon V75, so usage must be minimized. Solution: use FP16 throughout FlashAttention (16-bit in/out for exp, FP32 upcasting only for critical accumulation like row-wise sums), bounding the LUT to 65,536 entries (128 KiB); exploiting safe softmax (subtracting the row-max first, guaranteeing all exp inputs are non-positive) halves this to 32,768 entries (64 KiB, about 0.8% of total TCM), fitting vgather's addressing limits and precomputed once at system init with zero inference-time overhead. LUT-Centric Efficient Dequantization uses the vlut16 instruction (16-element table lookup per 8-bit source index) to directly convert 4-bit quantized values to FP16, avoiding the conventional mask-unpack-convert sequence and Hexagon's internal qfloat format conversion overhead on pre-V79 chips; the same approach easily supports other 4-bit encodings (FP4, NF4, IQ4_NL) by changing table contents. vlut16 is also used to broadcast quantization scales: since a 128-byte HVX register holds two FP16 groups of size 32, using four groups' scales as LUT content with predefined constant indices broadcasts four groups' scales in a single vlut16 instruction.

heading: Implementation
content: Built on top of llama.cpp, about 7K lines of C/C++ and inline assembly, using the LLVM toolchain in Hexagon SDK v6.0.0.2, with no dependency on Qualcomm's QNN, avoiding QNN's inflexible static fixed-shape computation graphs. Two modules: an NPU operator library (compute kernels, power management, hardware resource management, thread pool) compiled into an independent Hexagon DSP shared object; and a CPU-side module integrated into llama.cpp as a new Hexagon NPU backend, using rpcmem shared memory (a wrapper for kernel dmabuf, via libcdsprpc.so vendor libraries) to share physical memory between CPU and NPU without extra data copies, falling back to CPU execution for any operator not yet implemented on the NPU. Backend init calls the Hexagon SDK's FastRPC facility to start a remote NPU session; a polling thread on the NPU side receives computation requests from shared memory (lower latency than default RPC). Because CPU-NPU coherence on Snapdragon SoCs is one-way, the implementation manually clears CPU cache before NPU polling and for shared activation buffers.

heading: Evaluation
content: Devices: OnePlus Ace3 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 2, Hexagon V73), OnePlus 12 (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, Hexagon V75), OnePlus Ace5 Pro (Snapdragon 8 Elite, Hexagon V79); some accuracy results from an NVIDIA RTX3090 server testbed. Models: Qwen2.5 (1.5B, 3B, and 7B for cost-tradeoff analysis) and Llama 3.2 (1B, 3B); Skywork-1.5B-PRM as the outcome/process-reward scorer. Datasets: MATH500 and GSM8K (pass@1, 0-shot CoT) for test-time scaling accuracy; WinoGrande, MMLU, Wikitext-2 perplexity for general accuracy. Baseline: llama.cpp's OpenCL backend (GPU/Adreno); existing NPU systems (llm.npu, PowerServe, PowerInfer-2, HeteroLLM) were rejected as primary baselines for various limitations though QNN-based numbers are reported as reference. Key results: test-time scaling achieves a superior Pareto frontier, with Best-of-N on Qwen2.5 1.5B/3B outperforming base 3B/7B accuracy and Beam Search on Qwen2.5-1.5B/Llama3.2-1B matching or beating their own 3B variants; decoding throughput increases significantly with batch size across all three devices since idle HMX compute gets used at near-zero extra HMX time, though not perfectly linearly since lm_head stays on CPU due to the 32-bit NPU virtual address space limit; on OnePlus 12, decoding the 1.5B model stays under 5W and the 3B model stabilizes around 4.3W as batch size grows, and the 1.5B model at batch size 8 uses less energy than the 3B model at batch size 1 while matching its base math-task accuracy; the NPU system's decoding throughput and scaling at larger batch sizes beats GPU/OpenCL, and it consistently beats GPU on prefill throughput while approaching proprietary QNN FP16 under some workloads.

heading: Accuracy Assessment and Ablation Studies
content: Quantization scheme accuracy on Qwen2.5-1.5B: tile-quantization-group vs conventional grouping is close (WinoGrande 62.559 vs 63.349 vs F16's 64.613; MMLU 35.465 vs 35.271 vs F16's 34.819; Wiki PPL 10.206 vs 10.190 vs F16's 9.798), with differences much smaller than quantization's overall accuracy cost. LUT-based FP16 Attention vs conventional FP32 Attention shows no noticeable end-to-end accuracy difference (WinoGrande 62.796 vs 62.559; MMLU 35.207 vs 35.465; Wiki PPL 10.205 vs 10.206). Softmax ablation: LUT-based exp achieves 1.26-2.19x speedup vs F32 exp, up to 1.60x vs F16 exp, across query lengths 1/4/16 and KV lengths 1024/4096/16384, and is also more accurate than 16-bit polynomial approximation since precomputation can use higher precision. GEMM dequantization ablation: vs a naive baseline, the full optimized method (including quantization-group coalescing) achieves 9.65-19.04x speedup across matrix sizes; the HMX-layout-only variant (without coalescing) achieves 1.82-3.45x; the full method is only about 27% slower on average than a theoretical no-dequantization upper bound, indicating it is close to the ceiling. Overhead: on OnePlus 12, total memory consumption is about 1.3 GiB for the 1.5B model and about 2.4 GiB for the 3B model (4096-token context budget); CPU utilization rises with batch size (vocabulary-projection compute) but stays limited to 4 cores; decoding throughput declines only mildly as prompt length grows from 512 to 4096 tokens.

heading: Discussion: Generalizability and Limitations
content: Generalizability: the vector-plus-matrix NPU architecture pattern is increasingly universal, with modern CPUs (Intel AMX, ARM SME) adopting similar matrix units, and the gap between general-purpose and specialized low-precision matrix compute existing broadly including NVIDIA GPUs, suggesting the paper's core techniques generalize beyond Hexagon specifically. Limitations: decoding speed is still constrained mainly by dequantization overhead, since QNN-based quantized GEMM typically skips HVX entirely (DMA plus HMX only), and approaches like T-MAC could potentially enable more efficient fine-grained-quantized GEMV; prefill performance has room to improve via more NPU operator offloading, operator fusion, and better tiling/pipelining, left to future work; model size is currently bounded by the 32-bit virtual address space of a single NPU session on older devices, and multiple NPU sessions could help. Application scope: while parallel test-time scaling currently dominates math reasoning benchmarks, cited related work suggests extensibility to broader reasoning/planning domains.

heading: Related Work Summary
content: On-device LLM inference with NPUs: llm.npu (per-tensor INT8 GEMM, CPU-assisted outlier handling for prefill); HeteroLLM (GPU plus NPU collaborative inference via tensor partitioning); PowerServe (ONNX-intermediate quantized/floating-point partitioning); ShadowAttn (NPU plus CPU/GPU sparse attention); ExecuTorch (edge DNN framework supporting SpinQuant and QNN backend). All prior Hexagon-NPU-based work uses closed-source QNN as backend; MediaTek-NPU work is scarcer due to the non-public NeuroPilot SDK. LLM Quantization: GPTQ and AWQ are well-known post-training weight-only quantization methods; SmoothQuant and DuQuant extend to weight-activation quantization; QuaRot and SpinQuant are rotation-based methods quantizing weights, activations, and KV cache down to 4-bit. Speculative Decoding verifies multiple speculated tokens in one forward pass to address the memory-bound nature of decoding, and is theoretically part of the same generalized Generate-then-Verify framework as test-time scaling, which the authors say their system could support.

heading: Conclusion
content: The work demonstrates the feasibility and effectiveness of leveraging underutilized mobile NPU compute, specifically Qualcomm Hexagon, for LLM test-time scaling, via hardware-aware tile quantization, weight layout optimization, and LUT-based operator acceleration, showing smaller models with test-time scaling can outperform larger conventionally-deployed models in both accuracy and latency, advancing the Pareto frontier of on-device AI efficiency and capability.

heading: Relevance to This Project
content: Directly relevant to the on-device inference plan for Omni Claw/Novus Agenti as an engineering validation of running a capable model efficiently on a Qualcomm Hexagon NPU via QNN-adjacent or QNN-independent tooling. The paper's llama.cpp-based Hexagon NPU backend, built with no QNN dependency by reverse-engineering HMX instructions directly, is a notable alternative to the QNN-graph-partitioning approach described in the Omni Claw blueprint's ort_server daemon, worth comparing tradeoffs (QNN gives official support and easier development but is a black-box static graph; this paper's approach is unofficial/reverse-engineered but flexible and open). The tile-quantization and LUT-dequantization techniques are directly relevant to keeping a quantized on-device model both accurate and fast on Hexagon-class NPUs. The finding that decoding GEMM degenerates to GEMV, wasting matrix-unit capacity, and that batch-parallel workloads can reclaim that waste, is a reusable insight for any local multi-agent or multi-sample generation strategy on the same hardware class. github.com/haozixu/llama.cpp-npu is a concrete, research-grade alternative or reference implementation worth comparing against whatever NPU inference stack the eventual Omni Claw daemon uses.
